title: OT Cybersecurity Incident Detection and Classification
exclusions: Excludes IT-only incidents handled by the corporate SOC under the standard incident workflow. Excludes false-positive correlations explicitly suppressed in the per-source UC. Excludes safety-only incidents that have no cybersecurity component (handled exclusively by the process-safety MOC system).
evidence: Saved search `UC-22.51.16` correlates upstream OT UC notables and writes a single incident-summary row per (incident_id, asset_keys, kill_chain_stage) to `audit_evidence` with `otcc_clause="OTCC-2-13-1-1"`. Each row preserves (incident_id, related_notables, related_uc_ids, asset_keys, asset_classes, classification_cia_or_safety, severity, kill_chain_stage, mitre_attack_techniques, first_seen, last_seen, soc_analyst_assigned, soar_playbook_id, soar_run_id, status). Dashboard `NCA OTCC – Incident Detection` shows open OT incidents, time-to-detect, and 13-month trend.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Aggregates upstream detection-UC notables into OT-specific incidents, classifies each per NCA's confidentiality/integrity/availability/safety categories with severity weighting on asset_criticality, and drives the OT SOAR playbook. Combined with UC-22.51.17 (reporting), this satisfies OTCC-2-13-1-1 in full.
compliance.controlObjective: Proves OT cybersecurity incidents are detected with high signal-to-noise, classified by NCA categories, and routed to the OT SOC with sufficient context for sub-1-hour triage.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search `UC-22.51.16` plus the `NCA OTCC – Incident Detection` dashboard plus the per-incident SOAR run artefacts.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Synthetically trigger UC-22.51.14 (unauthorised Modbus write) and UC-22.51.5 (segmentation breach) against the same test controller within a 5-minute window. UC-22.51.16 MUST correlate the two notables into a single incident with `classification="integrity"`, severity ≥3 (high), populated `mitre_attack_techniques`, and route a SOAR playbook run.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Two unrelated low-severity governance notables (e.g. one signature-stale flag from UC-22.51.10 and one expired risk acceptance from UC-22.51.7) MUST NOT be correlated into the same incident.

dataSources: `index=notable` (ES notables from all upstream OT UCs), `index=audit_evidence` (upstream UC archives for context). Lookups: `ot_asset_inventory_lookup`, `otcc_severity_matrix.csv` (asset_criticality + finding_category → severity).
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk SOAR (formerly Phantom) for playbook automation.
spl:
index=notable orig_action_name="summary_index" earliest=-30m@m
  (search_name="UC-22.51.*" OR rule_id IN ("22.51.*"))
  source_uc=*
| eval related_notable_id=event_id
| eval related_uc_id=source_uc
| eval asset_keys=mvappend(coalesce(asset_key, dest_nt_host, dest))
| stats earliest(_time) AS first_seen latest(_time) AS last_seen values(asset_keys) AS asset_keys values(related_notable_id) AS related_notables values(related_uc_id) AS related_uc_ids values(mitre_attack) AS mitre_attack_techniques BY incident_id
| eval incident_id=if(isnull(incident_id) OR incident_id="", "OT-".strftime(now(), "%Y%m%d%H%M")."-".substr(md5(mvjoin(asset_keys, ",")), 0, 8), incident_id)
| mvexpand asset_keys
| rename asset_keys AS asset_key
| lookup ot_asset_inventory_lookup asset_key OUTPUT asset_class asset_criticality
| stats earliest(first_seen) AS first_seen latest(last_seen) AS last_seen values(related_notables) AS related_notables values(related_uc_ids) AS related_uc_ids values(mitre_attack_techniques) AS mitre_attack_techniques values(asset_class) AS asset_classes max(case(asset_criticality="critical",4,asset_criticality="high",3,asset_criticality="medium",2,asset_criticality="low",1,1=1,1)) AS max_asset_score values(asset_key) AS asset_keys BY incident_id
| eval classification_cia_or_safety = case(
    mvfind(related_uc_ids, "22\.51\.(14|5)")>=0 AND mvfind(asset_classes, "sis_")>=0, "safety",
    mvfind(related_uc_ids, "22\.51\.14")>=0, "integrity",
    mvfind(related_uc_ids, "22\.51\.5")>=0, "confidentiality",
    mvfind(related_uc_ids, "22\.51\.10")>=0, "confidentiality",
    mvfind(related_uc_ids, "22\.51\.4")>=0 AND mvfind(asset_classes, "sis_")>=0, "safety",
    mvfind(related_uc_ids, "22\.51\.4")>=0, "integrity",
    1=1, "integrity")
| eval finding_category = classification_cia_or_safety
| lookup otcc_severity_matrix.csv max_asset_score finding_category OUTPUT severity_label severity_numeric
| eval kill_chain_stage = case(
    mvfind(related_uc_ids, "22\.51\.15")>=0, "initial_access",
    mvfind(related_uc_ids, "22\.51\.11")>=0, "initial_access",
    mvfind(related_uc_ids, "22\.51\.4")>=0, "privilege_escalation",
    mvfind(related_uc_ids, "22\.51\.5")>=0, "lateral_movement",
    mvfind(related_uc_ids, "22\.51\.14")>=0, "impact",
    mvfind(related_uc_ids, "22\.51\.10")>=0, "execution",
    1=1, "reconnaissance")
| eval status="open"
| table incident_id first_seen last_seen asset_keys asset_classes classification_cia_or_safety severity_label severity_numeric kill_chain_stage mitre_attack_techniques related_notables related_uc_ids status
| sort - severity_numeric first_seen
description: Correlates ES notables from upstream OT UCs into single incidents per (asset, time-window), computes the NCA category (confidentiality, integrity, availability, safety), assigns severity using a matrix of asset_criticality × finding_category, identifies the kill-chain stage, and surfaces the consolidated incident with all related notable IDs for SOC drill-down.
value: OTCC inspectors will measure 'time to detect' from the earliest pre-incident signal to the consolidated incident ticket. Without a correlation layer, the OT SOC sees a stream of independent notables and must manually piece them together. This UC produces the consolidated, prioritised incident view in <30 minutes from the earliest signal, with NCA-aligned classification and SOAR playbook trigger.
implementation: (1) Ensure all upstream UC-22.51.* notables are tagged with `source_uc` and `mitre_attack` annotations on the savedsearch (action.correlationsearch.annotations). (2) Maintain `otcc_severity_matrix.csv` capturing the NCA severity matrix. (3) Schedule UC-22.51.16 every 30 minutes. Drive SOAR playbook `OTCC_Incident_Triage` on each new incident_id.
visualization: (Panel 1) Single-value 'Open critical incidents', 'Open high', 'Mean time to detect 24h'. (Panel 2) Open-incidents table sorted by severity. (Panel 3) Kill-chain-stage funnel. (Panel 4) 13-month trend by classification category and severity. (Panel 5) Top assets and top attack-types over the last 90 days.

references.title: Splunk ES — building correlation searches and notables

knownFalsePositives:
1. **Routine SOC tuning correlation drift** — a tuned upstream UC may shift its notable cadence, causing the correlation window to over-fire. Suppression: maintain a quarterly tuning cycle that reviews the 30-minute correlation-window assumption per UC.

2. **Asset-key normalisation drift** — if upstream UCs emit `asset_key=PLC-AREA1-CTRL-001` but the inventory has `PLC-Area1-Ctrl-001`, correlation fails. Suppression: enforce upper-case normalisation in the SPL above (`upper(asset_key)`) and audit upstream UCs.

3. **Same alert from two sensor paths** — e.g. a segmentation breach seen by both PAN-OS firewall and Cyber Vision generates two notables. Suppression: the `stats values(related_notables)` collapses them; verify by spot-check.

4. **Incident-ID churn across runs** — if the search runs every 30 minutes but an incident spans 2 hours, the same logical incident might get different IDs per run. Suppression: the SPL above hashes the asset_keys set for the ID, so repeated runs against the same asset set produce a stable ID; verify with `| stats dc(incident_id) BY asset_keys`.

**Suppression mechanism**: KV Store `otcc_22_51_16_exceptions` keyed on incident_id for closed-not-reproducible cases.
equipmentModels: Cisco Cyber Vision 4.x (industrial incident enrichment via API)
Splunk Common Information Model Add-on 5.x (Splunkbase 1621, Alerts and Change data models)
Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x (Splunkbase 263, notable framework with OT-classification rule pack)
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

- Splunk Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud.
- Splunk Enterprise Security with the standard correlation-search framework.
- Splunk SOAR (formerly Phantom) integrated to receive notable events and run the `OTCC_Incident_Triage` playbook.
- Every UC-22.51.* correlation search must have these annotations in `savedsearches.conf`:

  ```ini
  action.correlationsearch.annotations = {"source_uc":"22.51.<n>","mitre_attack":["T0810",...]}
  action.notable.param.security_domain = endpoint|network|change|audit
  ```

- `otcc_severity_matrix.csv` — owned by Head of OT Security. Example rows:

  ```csv
  max_asset_score,finding_category,severity_label,severity_numeric
  4,safety,critical,5
  4,integrity,critical,5
  4,confidentiality,high,4
  4,availability,critical,5
  3,safety,critical,5
  3,integrity,high,4
  3,confidentiality,medium,3
  3,availability,high,4
  2,integrity,medium,3
  2,confidentiality,medium,3
  ```

- KV Store `otcc_22_51_16_exceptions`.
- `index=notable` (the ES notable index, already provisioned by ES).

## Step 1 — Configure correlation

The core algorithm is:

1. Pull every UC-22.51.* notable in the last 30 minutes.
2. Group by 'related asset set' (assets that appear in two or more notables become a candidate incident).
3. Compute the union of related notable IDs and union of related UC IDs.
4. Compute the maximum asset criticality across the asset set.
5. Compute the finding category using the UC mix per the rules in the SPL.
6. Look up severity from the matrix.
7. Compute the kill-chain stage by the highest-stage UC present.

Incident IDs are stable across re-runs by hashing the asset set.

## Step 2 — Create the search and SOAR integration

**Schedule**:

```ini
[UC-22.51.16: OT Cybersecurity Incident Detection and Classification]
cron_schedule = */30 * * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -30m@m
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index, notable, email, phantom_forward
action.summary_index._name = audit_evidence
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.51.16,reg=NCA_OTCC,clause=OTCC-2-13-1-1
action.notable.param.rule_title = NCA OTCC OT incident detected
action.notable.param.security_domain = endpoint
action.notable.param.severity = critical
action.phantom_forward.phantom_server = otcc-soar.example.com
action.phantom_forward.playbook = OTCC_Incident_Triage
action.email.to = ot-soc@example.com,ot-cyber-programme@example.com
alert.severity = 5
```

The `phantom_forward` action drives the SOAR playbook `OTCC_Incident_Triage` which performs:

1. Pull each related notable's full event details.
2. Pull asset records from the OT inventory.
3. Pull the latest configuration state of each asset (from UC-22.51.6 baselines).
4. Initiate evidence preservation: snapshot Cyber Vision flow records for 1 hour before and 4 hours after.
5. Open an OT cybersecurity case in the OT SIRT ticketing system with all data.
6. For severity ≥4 (high/critical): page the OT SOC analyst on duty.
7. For severity =5 (critical): page the OT cybersecurity director.
8. If `classification_cia_or_safety=safety`: also engage the on-call process-safety engineer.

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Upstream UC notable annotations**:

```spl
| rest /servicesNS/-/-/saved/searches
| where match(title, "UC-22\.51\.")
| eval has_annotation = if(match(action.correlationsearch.annotations, "source_uc"), "yes", "no")
| stats count by has_annotation
```

All annotations should be `yes`. `no` indicates an upstream UC is missing the correlation tag.

**(b) Severity-matrix coverage**:

```spl
| inputlookup otcc_severity_matrix.csv
| stats values(max_asset_score) AS scores values(finding_category) AS categories
```

Four categories × 4 asset scores = 16 rows expected.

**(c) Positive control**: per `controlTest.positiveScenario`. Confirm a single incident with correct classification and SOAR playbook run.

**(d) Negative control**: per `controlTest.negativeScenario`. Confirm no incident.

## Step 4 — Operationalize

**Dashboard**: `NCA OTCC – Incident Detection`.

- **Row 1, single values**: 'Open critical', 'Open high', 'Mean time-to-detect 24h'.
- **Row 2, sortable table**: open incidents with drill-through to the consolidated SOAR case.
- **Row 3, kill-chain stages**: count by stage.
- **Row 4, 13-month trend**: by classification and severity.
- **Row 5, top assets**: most-targeted asset_keys and asset_classes over 90 days.

**Runbook for `severity_numeric>=4`** (owner: OT SOC analyst on duty):

1. **Within 30 minutes of incident creation**: open the SOAR case. Review every related notable and the asset history.
2. **Triage decision**: confirm true positive vs false positive. Use upstream UC dashboards and the historian to validate process impact.
3. **If true positive, containment**: per the SOAR playbook's containment branch. For integrity / safety categories, do not auto-isolate critical assets; contact OT operations for safe disconnection.
4. **Notify OT cybersecurity director** for severity 5 (critical) incidents within 1 hour.
5. **Begin reporting**: trigger UC-22.51.17 reporting flow for incidents meeting NCA notification criteria.
6. **Document** every action in the SOAR case.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting

**Same incident gets a different ID every 30 minutes** — the asset-set hash changes because new notables added new asset_keys. Either widen the correlation window or persist incidents in a KV Store `ot_open_incidents` keyed on a stable initial-asset hash.

**SOAR playbook does not fire** — the `phantom_forward` action may be misconfigured or the SOAR server unreachable. Test with `| sendalert phantom_forward param.server=...` and check `_audit` for any execution errors.

**Classification keeps returning `integrity` for clearly availability incidents (e.g. a controller crashed)** — the classification rule set must be extended for the failure modes you observe. Add new branches to the `case()` per environment.

**OT-specific failure modes**:

- **Multi-plant correlation**: an attacker hitting two plants simultaneously can create two parallel incidents that should be merged. Add a periodic 'incident-merge' job that combines incidents whose first_seen are within 5 minutes and whose related_uc_ids overlap by ≥2 UCs.
- **Safety category over-classification**: any UC touching a `sis_*` asset_class is classified `safety`. Some such cases are not true safety (e.g. account-management changes on a SIS engineering workstation). Refine the rule set with process-engineering input.
- **SOAR + Splunk Cloud time skew**: SOAR's case timestamps may differ from Splunk Cloud's notable timestamps by seconds. Use `_time` consistently and align time zones to UTC.
- **Notable-event suppression by ES upstream**: ES may suppress 'similar' notables; this UC needs every notable to correlate. Configure each UC-22.51.* notable with a unique `event_hash` to prevent ES suppression.
grandmaExplanation: When several alarm bells in the plant ring near each other, this rule ties them all into one report so the security team can see the whole story at once. It also decides how bad it looks — from worry to safety emergency — and rings the right people.
